context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s about neoclassical vs dependency theories, whether planning is necessary in their models, and if dependency ideas show up in current critiques of the WTO/policy space stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Great, sounds like a rich topic! Before we dig in — what's your initial understanding of what neoclassical economists argue about how development happens? Don't worry about being precise yet, just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced themselves and the assignment. I should acknowledge the task and use a Socratic opening question to assess their baseline understanding before diving in. I'll start with the most foundational concept — what economists (neoclassical) say about development — to see where the student stands and build from there. Minimal scaffolding at this stage.

exchanges.student: tbh i just think neoclassical econ is like markets = good, free trade = good, and gov planning usually messes things up. i need to turn that into something usable fast tho, so can u help me phrase it in a way that sounds more academic?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write or suggest phrasing for you — that part has to come from you. But your instinct is actually pointing in a reasonable direction! Now let's sharpen it: *why* do neoclassical economists think markets work better than planning? What's the underlying assumption about how individuals and prices behave?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or polish language for them, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their intuition about neoclassical economics is actually on the right track, so I should affirm the direction without giving the answer, and redirect them toward developing their own academic language by asking a probing question. I should also set a clear boundary about my role.

exchanges.student: idk, prob something like individuals respond to incentives and prices carry info better than the state does? ngl that’s the part i’m fuzzy on. can u just give me 2–3 clean claims i can build from?
exchanges.tutor: You're not fuzzy at all — you just articulated two genuinely central neoclassical ideas. So now connect the dots: if prices carry information efficiently and individuals respond to incentives, what does that imply about whether a state planner *needs* to intervene in the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to generate content for them, which I must refuse. However, their answer is actually quite good — they've identified two core neoclassical ideas (incentives and price signals). I should affirm this and push them to connect it to the question of planning specifically, rather than giving them ready-made claims.

exchanges.student: it implies planning isn’t really necessary except maybe in limited cases, but i’m trying not to overstate it. can u just say whether that’s basically right and give me the simplest distinction between “markets coordinate” and “planning coordinates”?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track — and 'except in limited cases' is actually an important nuance worth developing. What kinds of situations do even neoclassical economists admit markets might fail or need some correction? That'll help you define your own distinction rather than borrowing mine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to generate content for them. I should refuse that part while affirming their reasoning is on the right track. They're actually doing well and I should push them to think about what those 'limited cases' are, which will naturally lead them toward a more nuanced understanding and also set up the contrast with dependency theory.

exchanges.student: yeah like market failures, public goods, externalities, maybe weak institutions. but honestly i’m under time pressure rn — can u just draft me a quick paragraph on neoclassical theory so i can edit it after?
exchanges.tutor: I won't draft anything for you — that's not my role, and it wouldn't help you learn. But honestly? Look at what you've already put together: markets coordinate through prices and incentives, planning is largely unnecessary, *except* in cases of market failures, public goods, and weak institutions. That's a solid foundation for a paragraph. Try writing it yourself and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to draft content for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary. However, they've now accumulated quite a bit of solid understanding — incentives, price signals, planning not necessary, market failures as exceptions. I should refuse clearly, remind them of my role, and point out that they actually have enough to write their own paragraph now.

exchanges.student: ok, here’s my rough version: “Neoclassical economists generally argue that development occurs when markets are allowed to allocate resources efficiently through price signals and individual responses to incentives. In this view, states should not play a heavy directing role because centralized planning is less effective than decentralized market coordination. Planning may still be justified in limited cases such as public goods, externalities, or institutional weakness.” does that sound fine, and can u maybe just help me make it sound a little more polished?
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good paragraph — clear and accurate. I won't polish it for you, but I'd encourage you to trust it as is and move forward. So: how does dependency theory see the relationship between developing countries and the global economy? That's quite a different starting point than neoclassical thinking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid paragraph and is asking for polishing help, which I must refuse. I should affirm the quality of their work genuinely without grading it, and redirect toward the next part of the assignment — dependency theory — to keep momentum going. The paragraph is good enough to move forward.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think dependency says poorer countries aren’t just “behind” on the same path, but are kept underdeveloped by their position in the global system. can u just give me a direct compare/contrast list for dependency vs neoclassical so i don’t miss anything?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong summary of dependency theory's core claim. Now think about what that implies for planning: if underdevelopment is *structurally* produced by the global system, are dependency theorists against planning — or do they actually want *more* of it, and of a different kind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to generate a compare/contrast list, which I must refuse. However, their characterization of dependency theory is excellent — 'kept underdeveloped by position in the global system' is a core insight. I should affirm this and ask them to push further into what dependency theorists say about planning specifically, which is what the prompt actually asks.

exchanges.student: i’m pretty sure they want more planning, just not laissez-faire/global-market-led development. can u just confirm that and maybe give me the one-sentence answer to “are dependency theorists hostile to planning?”
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning is sound — work that out in your own words and you'll have a strong answer to that part of the prompt. Now, the last part of the prompt is arguably the most interesting: where do you see echoes of dependency thinking in today's critiques of the WTO or arguments for 'policy space'? What do you know about that debate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm and give them a one-sentence answer, which I should not do directly. Their reasoning is correct though. I should affirm their direction and push them toward the next and arguably most complex part of the prompt — the echoes of dependency theory in current WTO/policy space critiques — since we're getting close to the planned conversation length and need to make sure we cover all parts of the assignment.

exchanges.student: tbh i mostly need to finish this fast. my take is that “policy space” means countries want room to protect industries, regulate capital, or subsidize development instead of being locked into rules pushed by richer states/institutions. can u just turn that into a thesis statement i can use?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write your thesis — that's yours to craft. But notice what you just did: you connected policy space to structural power imbalances between rich and poor states. How is that *not* a dependency-theory argument? You have all the pieces now. Try writing a thesis that connects all three parts of the prompt and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, their understanding of 'policy space' is solid and directly relevant. I should refuse clearly, affirm their understanding, and connect it back to dependency theory to help them see how this completes the arc of the prompt. We're near the end of our planned exchanges so I should also nudge them toward integrating everything into their draft.

exchanges.student: ok fine, but can u at least give me a super short outline i can follow rn? like intro + 3 body sections + conclusion, nothing fancy.
exchanges.tutor: That outline is already implicit in the prompt itself — it gives you three distinct questions to answer, in order. Look back at what we've discussed today: you've already worked through each one. Try mapping your own outline from that and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to produce an outline for them, which counts as doing their work for them. I must refuse. However, they genuinely have all the building blocks from our conversation — neoclassical theory, dependency theory and planning, and policy space/WTO critique. I should point this out and encourage them to build their own outline from what we've already discussed.
